Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd tell myself I was going to sit down and work, but then I'd just end up scrolling on my phone or doing something else. When I did study, it was usually hands-on stuff like working through practice problems or trying to physically write things out. I know I'm enrolled in like ten courses, but organizing my time across all of them felt overwhelming, so I mostly just reacted to whatever felt most urgent.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I definitely wasn't one of those people who studied at the same time every day. If a deadline was coming up, I'd panic and cram, but otherwise I'd drift through the week without much structure. Some weeks I'd put in decent hours, other weeks almost nothing. It really depended on my anxiety levels and whether something scared me enough to act.
Q3: Usually fear, to be honest. I'd log in when I suddenly remembered an assignment was due or when I got that panicked feeling that I was falling behind. Sometimes a classmate would mention something in a group chat and I'd rush to check if I'd missed it. It wasn't a regular habit—more like damage control when the anxiety hit hard enough to push me into action.
Q4: Not really, no. I never got to the point where logging in felt automatic or normal. There were brief moments where I'd tell myself I was going to check in daily, but that never lasted more than a few days. The peer support stuff helped a bit because other students would remind me about things, but even then it didn't become a real routine for me.
Q5: The videos were actually helpful when I watched them because I could pause and replay parts I didn't get. I learn better when I can see things being done rather than just reading about them. The discussion boards were okay too because sometimes other students would explain things in ways that clicked better for me. Anything interactive or practical felt more useful than walls of text.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials, honestly. Long PDFs and articles just made my eyes glaze over. I'd open them, scroll a bit, then close them and tell myself I'd come back later. I also barely touched the optional quizzes because they felt like extra work when I was already struggling to keep up with the required stuff. If something didn't seem immediately necessary, it got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to an assignment or exam. If I could tell it would help me not fail, I'd do it. Also, anything that involved actually doing something rather than just passively reading felt more valuable. I needed to feel like I was making progress, not just consuming information that might or might not matter.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. When you're already anxious about completing the required work, optional activities just feel like extra pressure. I'd look at them and think 'that might be helpful' but then immediately prioritize whatever was mandatory. Required activities had consequences attached, so they won, even if the optional ones might have been more interesting.
Q9: Definitely. I downloaded a lot of materials and then studied them on my tablet without being connected to anything. I also used YouTube a lot to find explanations of concepts because sometimes random creators explained things better than the official course stuff. I'd take handwritten notes too, which helped me remember things better since I'm a hands-on learner. None of that showed up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was working through problems on paper or rewatching downloaded videos, and the LMS had no idea. I'd also study with friends from my peer support group in person, working through stuff together at someone's apartment. We'd quiz each other and explain concepts, but that collaborative learning was completely invisible online. My best studying often happened away from the computer.
Q11: All the time. I'd download lecture slides or videos once, then never open them again through the LMS. I preferred having things saved locally so I could access them without internet or logging in. It felt less overwhelming to study from my own organized folders than to navigate through the course platform every time. One download could turn into hours of offline study.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when everything seemed to pile up at once. My anxiety would spike and I'd feel paralyzed—like I had so much to do that I couldn't start anything. The fear of failing made me avoid looking at the course entirely sometimes, which I know sounds backwards. When I felt most scared was actually when I engaged the least.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired or had other stuff going on, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. Confusion was a big one too—if I didn't understand something, I'd avoid it rather than ask for help. Having ten courses meant there was always something competing for my attention, so the courses where I felt most lost got pushed to the bottom of the list. Fatigue made everything feel impossible.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd be logged in and clicking through stuff but not actually absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere, worrying about other things or just zoning out. I'd watch videos at 2x speed just to say I'd watched them, not really learning anything. There were times I'd read the same paragraph multiple times and still have no idea what it said because my brain was fried.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially when they felt too big or confusing. The bigger and scarier an assignment seemed, the more I'd avoid it until the last possible moment. I knew it was important, but the anxiety made me want to not think about it. It's like my brain preferred the temporary relief of avoidance over the stress of actually starting. I'd distract myself with easier tasks or just escape entirely.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me engage sometimes. I'd ignore something for weeks, then panic-work the night before it was due. My LMS activity probably looked like nothing, nothing, nothing, then a huge spike right before submissions. It wasn't healthy, but the pressure of a deadline was often the only thing strong enough to overcome my avoidance. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have done anything.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it did affect me, but I didn't always check it right away. Good feedback made me feel like maybe I could do this, which helped my mood. Negative feedback was harder—sometimes it made me more anxious and I'd avoid that course even more. The peer support helped because other students would encourage me to look at feedback and not take it too personally.
Q18: The structure felt kind of overwhelming with so many courses running at once. When things were clearly laid out with specific deadlines, I could at least panic-plan around them. But when the structure was loose or self-paced, I'd just drift and fall behind. I needed external pressure because my own motivation was basically nonexistent. More structure would have helped me, honestly.
Q19: Having everything spread across different sections and modules made it hard to know what to prioritize. I wished there was like a simple checklist showing exactly what I needed to do and when. The discussion boards were actually good because they made me feel less alone. But some of the course design assumed I had more self-discipline than I actually do, which made engagement harder.
Q20: I can totally see how that happens. You might be logging in constantly, clicking around, trying to understand what you need to do, but never actually completing anything because you're confused or anxious. Or you're using the LMS to access materials you're studying elsewhere. High activity doesn't mean productive activity—sometimes it's just spinning your wheels or avoiding the actual work.
Q21: That kind of describes me, actually. My attendance and LMS activity look terrible, but I still managed a Merit grade because I studied a lot offline and crammed effectively before exams. I'm decent at taking tests when I actually know the material, even if my process getting there looks chaotic. The LMS doesn't capture midnight study sessions with downloaded notes or last-minute peer study groups.
Q22: Course logs can show when you clicked on things and how long you had them open, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention or learning. They miss all the offline work, the thinking you do while walking around, the conversations with classmates. They also can't capture your mental state—like whether you were engaged or just going through the motions because you were terrified of failing.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it somehow. It's not just doing the work—it's wanting to understand it. For me, real engagement happened in short bursts, usually when something clicked or when I was working with other people. It's hard to feel engaged when you're anxious all the time, even if you technically show up and do things.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was most of the time, or how much the peer support actually helped me get through this. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying offline or the mental energy it took just to open my laptop some days. It also wouldn't show that despite my low motivation and messy habits, I actually did learn things—just not in the neat, trackable way the system expects.
